g.data-narration: Welcome to Electronic Mail - the foundational application layer protocol that revolutionized digital communication worldwide.
g.text: university 2022 Scheme - CSE | Application Layer Protocols

g.data-narration: Email is a method of exchanging digital messages between users over a network using standardized protocols like SMTP, POP3, and IMAP.
g.text: A method of exchanging digital messages between users over a network using standardized protocols.
Operates at the Application Layer of TCP/IP, using store-and-forward mechanism for reliable delivery.

g.data-narration: Email is essential in networking as it demonstrates client-server architecture, protocol design, and distributed system concepts.
g.text: Demonstrates fundamental networking concepts including protocol design, message formatting,
client-server architecture, and distributed systems challenges.

g.data-narration: The email system has four key components: MUA for user interface, MTA for routing, MDA for delivery, and MRA for retrieval.
SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) is the standard protocol for sending emails, using TCP port 25 for server-to-server communication.
g.text: Standard protocol for sending and forwarding emails across IP networks.
Operates on client-server model using text-based commands and response codes.

g.data-narration: SMTP uses commands like HELO, MAIL FROM, RCPT TO, DATA, and QUIT for email transmission between mail servers.
POP3 (Post Office Protocol v3) is a simple email retrieval protocol that downloads messages and typically deletes them from the server.
g.text: Simple protocol for retrieving emails from server.
Downloads messages and typically deletes from server.

g.data-narration: IMAP allows users to manage emails on the server, supporting folder organization and multi-device synchronization.
g.text: Advanced email management directly on server.
Supports folder organization, search, and multi-device sync.
Changes sync across all devices automatically.
Ideal for smartphones, tablets, multiple computers.

g.data-narration: POP3 is simple but deletes messages after download, while IMAP keeps messages on server for synchronized multi-device access.
Email messages consist of header fields (From, To, Subject) and body, separated by a blank line, following RFC 822 standard.
g.text: Supports: HTML, images, audio, video, attachments

g.data-narration: Email addresses follow username@domainname format, where domain maps to DNS MX records for mail routing.
The complete email flow shows how messages travel from sender MUA through multiple MTAs to recipient's mailbox.
Key protocols summary: SMTP (port 25) for sending, POP3 (port 110) for simple retrieval, IMAP (port 143) for advanced management.
Remember: Email uses MUA-MTA-MDA-MRA architecture, SMTP for sending, POP3/IMAP for retrieval, and MIME for rich content support.
